race.name: Haute Route Dolomites
race.tagline: 450 kilometers and 14,500 meters of climbing across 5 stages through the Italian Dolomites and Alps — Stelvio, Gavia, Pordoi, and the jagged spires of UNESCO World Heritage mountains. Professional stage-race format for amateur cyclists who want to suffer like the pros.

vitals.date: Historically late August / early September (check hauteroute.org for 2026 status)
vitals.date_specific: 2026: DISCONTINUED. Haute Route collapsed to Alps-only since 2025 (now run by French Velo Evenements/FVE). Dolomites edition last ran in 2023.
vitals.registration: Via hauteroute.org when event is scheduled. Historically part of the Haute Route Series. Entry includes full logistics, feed stations, timing, mechanical support, post-stage meals, and luggage transport. The Haute Route Dolomites was a 5-stage event from Cortina d'Ampezzo to Bormio (or reverse). Currently not offered as a separate event — Haute Route focuses on the Alps.
vitals.entry_fee: EUR 900-1,500 (varies by registration phase; includes logistics, feed stations, timing, post-stage meals, luggage transport)
vitals.feed_zones: Multiple feed stations per stage with water, energy food, and mechanical support. Mavic neutral support vehicles on course. Post-stage hot meal included. Massage and recovery services at finish village.
vitals.cutoff_time: Daily cut-off times enforced — typically 8-10 hours per stage
route_options: Full Course: ~450 km / 14,500m across 5 stages (4 road stages + 1 time trial)
No shorter options within the event — riders must complete all stages for GC classification

climate.primary: Late-summer Alpine — warm valleys, frigid high passes, rapid weather changes
climate.description: Late August and early September in the Italian Dolomites and Alps brings valley temperatures of 20-28C (68-82F) with dramatically cooler conditions at altitude. The Stelvio Pass at 2,758m and the Gavia at 2,621m regularly see temperatures near or below freezing even in late summer — snow on the Stelvio summit is not unusual in August. The Dolomite passes between 2,000-2,400m are 10-15C cooler than valley floors. Alpine weather is notoriously volatile: clear morning conditions can deteriorate into thunderstorms, rain, and fog within hours, particularly above 2,000m. The 2023 edition had its opening stage modified due to snow on the high passes.
challenges: Stelvio (2,758m) and Gavia (2,621m) can see near-freezing temperatures and snow even in late August
20-25C temperature swings between valley floors and pass summits within a single stage
Five consecutive days of mountain riding compounds fatigue and cold exposure
Alpine thunderstorms can develop rapidly above 2,000m — lightning risk on exposed passes
Wet descents at altitude after summit finishes in rain or fog — reduced visibility and cold hands

terrain.primary: Paved Alpine and Dolomite mountain pass roads
terrain.surface: Entirely paved on Italian state roads, provincial roads, and military roads through the Dolomites and eastern Alps. The legendary passes — Stelvio (48 hairpins on the Bormio side), Gavia, Mortirolo, Pordoi, Falzarego — are classic Giro d'Italia roads with generally good surface quality. The Gavia road surface is noticeably rougher and narrower than others, with tunnels and rock overhangs that feel more like mountain adventure than road cycling. Some stages include lesser-known passes with less traffic and slightly variable surface quality. All climbing is on tarmac — no gravel, no off-road.
features: Passo dello Stelvio from Bormio: 21.5 km at 7.1%, 48 hairpins, summit at 2,758m — highest paved pass in the Eastern Alps
Passo Gavia: 17.3 km at 7.9% from Ponte di Legno, summit at 2,621m — narrow, exposed, and savage
Passo Pordoi: 9.4 km at 6.8% from Arabba, summit at 2,239m — classic Giro d'Italia Cima Coppi
Passo Falzarego: 15 km at 5.5%, summit at 2,105m — panoramic Dolomite approach to Cortina
Individual time trial on a signature climb — typically 10-20 km uphill
Daily stages averaging 90 km with 2,500-3,500m of climbing

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 83. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 80.

course_description.character: A multi-stage traverse of the Italian Dolomites and eastern Alps, linking the glamour of Cortina d'Ampezzo to the alpine spa town of Bormio through the most legendary passes in Italian cycling. The route deliberately targets the giants — Stelvio, Gavia, Pordoi, Falzarego — and connects them with lesser-known climbs that are equally demanding but less crowded. Each stage averages 90 km with 2,500-3,500m of climbing, creating a daily rhythm of sustained mountain suffering punctuated by breathtaking descents through UNESCO World Heritage Dolomite scenery.
course_description.signature_challenge: The Stelvio from Bormio: 21.5 km at 7.1% with 48 switchbacks to 2,758m — arriving on Stage 4 with 10,000+ meters of climbing already in the legs and 12-18% less oxygen at the summit. The highest paved pass in the Eastern Alps, climbed when you are at your most depleted.

logistics.airport: Venice Marco Polo (VCE) 160 km from Cortina (~2 hr drive). Milan Malpensa (MXP) 200 km from Bormio (~3 hr drive). Milan Bergamo (BGY) 170 km from Bormio (~2.5 hr drive). Haute Route provides designated airport transfers (included or available at extra cost).
logistics.lodging_strategy: Haute Route offers accommodation packages with hotels in stage towns. Independent accommodation is available in Cortina (EUR 100-250/night), Bormio (EUR 80-200/night), and intermediate stage towns. The multi-stage format means you need accommodation in 4-5 different locations unless using Haute Route's logistics package. Cortina is the most expensive — a glamorous resort town. Bormio is more moderate.
logistics.food: Italian mountain cuisine: polenta, game, alpine cheeses, local wines from Trentino-Alto Adige and Lombardy. Haute Route provides post-stage hot meals included in entry. On-course feed stations supply energy food and water. Stage towns have excellent local restaurants. The culinary progression from Dolomite South Tyrolean cuisine (speck, canederli) in Cortina to Lombardian specialties (bresaola, pizzoccheri) in Bormio mirrors the geographic journey.

history.origin_story: The Haute Route Dolomites was launched in 2014 as an expansion of the Haute Route Series beyond its original French Alps flagship event (founded 2011). The concept was to bring the same professional-level multi-day stage race format to the Italian Dolomites and eastern Alps — home to the most legendary climbs of the Giro d'Italia. The inaugural route connected Geneva to Venice via the Swiss Alps and Dolomites in a 7-day format. Later editions evolved to a 5-day format focused on the Dolomites (typically Cortina to Bormio or similar), incorporating the Stelvio, Gavia, and the signature Dolomite passes. The event gained global attention through its association with the Netflix documentary 'Icarus' (2017), which was filmed at a Haute Route event. The intimate field size (200-400 riders) and the dramatic Dolomite setting created a loyal following among serious amateur mountain cyclists.
notable_moments: 2014: Inaugural Haute Route Dolomites — 7-day format from Geneva to Venice via Swiss Alps and Dolomites
2017: Netflix documentary 'Icarus' releases — filmed at a Haute Route event, raising global awareness
2022: 5-stage format from Cortina to Bormio — 450 km and 14,500m of climbing across the Stelvio, Gavia, and Dolomite passes
2023: Weather disruption — opening stage modified due to snow on high passes, highlighting the altitude challenges
Haute Route Series evolves — Alps remains flagship, Dolomites and other formats adjusted based on demand
Two Wheel Tours (Australia) announces independent 'Best of the Dolomites' tour after Haute Route Dolomites discontinuation
history.reputation: Regarded as the most demanding Haute Route event due to the combination of the Stelvio and Gavia passes with the Dolomite's distinctive climbing profile. Cycling media and participant reviews consistently describe it as a transformative experience that combines the severity of the highest Alpine passes with the visual beauty of the Dolomites.

biased_opinion.summary:
The Haute Route Dolomites is the most demanding amateur multi-day cycling event in Italy. 450 km. 14,500 meters of climbing. 5 stages through the Stelvio (2,758m), Gavia (2,621m), Pordoi, Falzarego, and the full catalogue of Dolomite passes that define the Giro d'Italia. This is not a tourism ride with mountains in the background — it is a professional-format stage race where the mountains are trying to break you.

The Stelvio is the signature: 21.5 km at 7.1% with 48 switchbacks on the Bormio side, summiting at 2,758m where the air has 12-18% less oxygen. The Gavia is the wild card: 17.3 km at 7.9% on a narrow, rough road through alpine wilderness that feels more like an expedition than a bike race. The Dolomite stages deliver the visual drama — vertical limestone towers, meadow-filled valleys, and the unique pink glow of the enrosadira at sunset. Five days of this creates a complete experience that is equal parts athletic challenge and mountain pilgrimage.

The Haute Route organization makes it possible. Feed stations, mechanical support, timing, luggage transport, and post-stage meals remove the logistical complexity that would otherwise make this route inaccessible to amateur riders. The 200-400 rider field creates genuine stage-race camaraderie — by the final stage, you have shared enough suffering to build real bonds. The price is high, the climbing is relentless, and the altitude is genuine. But the experience of riding the Stelvio and Gavia in a professionally supported stage race through UNESCO World Heritage Dolomite scenery is worth every euro and every meter of vertical gain.
strengths: 14,500m of climbing in 5 days including the Stelvio (2,758m) and Gavia (2,621m) — the highest passes in the Eastern Alps
Professional Haute Route organization: feed stations, Mavic support, timing, luggage transport, post-stage meals
UNESCO Dolomite scenery — vertical limestone towers, alpine meadows, and Giro d'Italia pass history
Intimate 200-400 rider field creates genuine stage-race community and GC competition
Complete professional stage-race experience: daily rankings, time trials, and GC classification
Netflix 'Icarus' connection raises the event's profile in cycling culture
weaknesses: EUR 900-1,500 entry fee plus accommodation makes this one of the most expensive amateur events
Event future uncertain — Haute Route has consolidated around the Alps format, Dolomites may not run annually
Altitude at 2,600-2,758m causes real physiological impact — 12-18% power reduction at Stelvio summit
Five consecutive days of extreme climbing demands months of specific preparation
Remote Dolomite start/finish locations require complex travel logistics
Cold and snow risk at altitude even in late August — the 2023 edition was weather-affected
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you want to ride the greatest mountain passes in Italian cycling in a professionally supported stage-race format, the Haute Route Dolomites is the definitive experience. The Stelvio and Gavia alone justify the entry. The Dolomite stages add visual drama that no Alpine event can match. Confirm the event is running before committing — and train specifically for altitude, as the Stelvio and Gavia will humble any rider who arrives unprepared.

final_verdict.one_liner: 450 km and 14,500m of climbing through the Stelvio, Gavia, and the full Dolomite pass collection in 5 days — the most demanding amateur stage race in Italy.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Ride this if you are a serious mountain cyclist seeking a multi-day challenge through the most iconic passes in Italian cycling. Skip it if you are on a tight budget, cannot commit to months of altitude-specific climbing preparation, or prefer gravel/mixed terrain. Confirm the event is scheduled before planning — Haute Route's calendar has evolved.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a 7-day Alps challenge: Haute Route Alps (800 km, 20,000m). For a single-day Dolomite gran fondo: Maratona dles Dolomites (138 km, 4,190m). For independent Dolomite touring: self-organized route through the same passes. For the Stelvio specifically: Stelvio Bike Day (car-free) or the Gran Fondo Stelvio Santini.

videos.title: Haute Route Dolomites: Stage 4 Highlights
videos.description: It’s all about Stelvio. In the penultimate stage of the race, we rode the legendary loop from Bormio over Umbria Pass into Switzerland, before tackling the fearsome Stelvio. Taking us to the highest point of the entire 2021 race in style, this stage was an all-time classic. Enjoy the highlights.

videos.title: Highlights: 2021 Haute Route Dolomites
videos.description:
Conquering legendary cols, immersed in the raw beauty of the Dolomites, the 2021 edition of the Haute Route Dolomites was one to remember. Racing 400km from Cortina to Bormio, the peloton earned over 14,000m of elevation over five intense days in the saddle. Steep and stunning climbs, incredible vistas and charming villages: this is what riding the Dolomites is all about. 
Enjoy the highlights from the 2021 Haute Route Dolomites.

videos.title: Italy’s Ultimate Bike Ride - GCN’s Dolomite Epic
videos.description:
When you think about cycling, Italy is a country that always stands out. The racing history, scenery and iconic brands all make it an epic place to ride your bike. We went to the Dolomites, the ultimate cycling destination, for a 120km dream ride over four legendary mountain passes: the Pordoi, Giau, Falzarego and Fedaia. This is Italy: GCN’s perfect ride!

In association with @CampagnoloSrl 👉 https://gcn.eu/Campagnolo
In association with @colnagoTV 👉 https://gcn.eu/Colnago

This route and loads more are available on Komoot, the Giro's official mapping partner:
GCN'S Dolomite Epic Route 👉 https://gcn.eu/ItalyEpicRoute
All stages of the Giro d'Italia 👉 https://gcn.eu/KomootGiro

Watch the in-depth bike check over on @gcntech 👉 https://gcntech.co/ItalyBikeCheck

Useful Links:

Which climbs would you choose to tackle on your perfect Italian ride? Let us know in the comments below! 💬

If you liked this video, be sure to give it a thumbs up 👍 and share it with your friends!

Watch more on GCN...

The Global Cycling Network (GCN) is the largest online cycling channel in the world, bringing together a global community of road cyclists to celebrate everything that’s great about the world of cycling.

Our videos bring fans compelling daily content including expert tutorials, techniques, training, racing, cutting-edge bike tech, unparalleled behind the scenes event coverage, humour,  entertainment, and more.

Presented by ex-pro riders, GCN offers a uniquely qualified insight into the world of cycling, and most importantly it’s fuelled by our passionate and enthusiastic fans – everyone who makes up the GCN community. We also bring the latest and greatest tech to your attention, showcase the best places in the world to ride and get exclusive access to events and races.

Welcome to the Global Cycling Network | Inside Cycling

videos.title: Road bike climbs: Stelvio Pass - unedited version, Italy (from Bormio) - Haute Route 2016
videos.description:
Filmed during Haute Route Dolomites - 2016
This version has been added by popular demand
Edited version: www.youtube.com/watch?v=AcHnllp1bZs&t=66s
Strava: https://www.strava.com/activities/704...
Relive.cc: https://www.relive.cc/view/704819557
videos.transcript:
[Music]
see
Allen from Karla to
you
videos.curation_reason: Immersive unedited first-person POV of the iconic Stelvio climb filmed during actual Haute Route competition.

quotes.text: Sometimes there's still 15k to go and you're already tired so you have to trust your form, trust your mind and know that kilometer by kilometer you will get to the top. It's the hardest day in my life but it's something different, the biggest challenge I can find.

Everybody encourages everybody else, it's a unique event from that perspective - we're all competing against each other but we all want to succeed together. You're now riding as one with a big group of friends, experiencing the life you were dreaming about just a few months ago.

key_challenges.name: Passo Pordoi
key_challenges.description: 30 kilometers long with average of 6 percent gradient, over 2000 meters high and 28 switchbacks. First climb straight out the back with no warm up.

terrain_notes.text: Described as the tarmac dreams are made of with epic climbs providing breathtaking scenery and exhilarating descents.
The route from Canaze up Passo Pordoi is considered the more beautiful side compared to the shorter steeper opposite side used in the Giro.

race_day_tips.text: Trust your form and mind when you still have 15k to go and are already tired - know that kilometer by kilometer you will get to the top.

additional_quotes.text: There's no way to turn back it's time to go everybody encourages everybody else it's a unique event from that perspective we're all competing against each other but we all want to succeed together

I loved it the hardest day in my life it's something different it's not what i do in my daily life so and it's the biggest challenge i can find so i think i use it to prove to myself that this is yeah that i can achieve things

rider_intel.search_text: The Haute Route Dolomites features iconic climbs including the formidable Passo Pordoi as the opening challenge - 30 kilometers long with 6% average gradient, 28 switchbacks, and over 2000 meters elevation with no warm-up. The route covers 120 kilometers with four iconic Dolomiti climbs totaling over 3000 meters of elevation, including the Giau, Falzarego, and Fedaya passes. Riders describe the tarmac as dreams are made of with breathtaking scenery and exhilarating descents. The event creates a unique atmosphere where competitors encourage each other while racing together. Conditions can be extremely hot, and riders describe it as the hardest day of their lives requiring mental strength to push through when tired with 15k still remaining.
